## TIER 7 — Parables (Complete Readings)
*Full literary + theological readings of Quranic parables. No single entity hub owns these — they tag multiple entities and appear in multiple hubs. Long format (15-25 min) appropriate.*

- [ ] **"The Two Garden Owners: Being vs. Having in Al-Kahf"** (18:32-44, Baldwin/Berger hybrid, ~20min) — The richest parable in the Quran. One man defines himself by his garden; the other by his relationship to God. Enacts the being-vs-having distinction philosophically before it was named by existentialists. Tags: al-kahf, zulm, tawakkul, iman.
- [ ] **"The Town of Three Messengers: What Ya-Sin Says About Moral Courage"** (36:13-27, Dillard, ~15min) — Two messengers become three; the man who runs from across the city; the lone voice before execution. The Quran's portrait of individual witness against collective rejection.
- [ ] **"The Man Who Passed a Ruined Town: Al-Baqarah 2:259"** (Davenport, ~12min) — One of the most compressed miracle scenes in the Quran. Death, resurrection, a hundred years, a donkey, fresh dates. What the Quran is actually arguing through the scene.
- [ ] **"The Parable of the Word: Good Tree, Bad Tree (Ibrahim 14:24-26)"** (Izutsu, ~12min) — *Kalima tayyiba* as a tree with roots in the ground and branches in the sky. The Quran's most sustained visual metaphor for theological concepts.
- [ ] **"The Spider's House: What Al-Ankabut's Opening Parable Teaches About Fragile Systems"** (29:41, Berger, ~10min) — *Awhan al-buyut* — the most fragile of houses. What the Quran means by structural weakness disguised as protection.

---

## TIER 8 — Philosophy, Aqida, and Theology
*Articles that bring Quranic teaching into conversation with Islamic intellectual tradition, classical philosophy, and comparative theology. Can draw on Ibn Arabi, Al-Ghazali, Al-Farabi, neoplatonism, kalam, etc. — but the Quran is always the primary text and the conversation partner, not the footnote. Long format as needed.*

### Ontology and Existence
- [ ] **"What the Quran Says About Existence: Before Wujud Was a Term"** (Berger/Izutsu, ~20min, long-form) — The Quran's treatment of being and non-being: *kun fa-yakun*, *la hawla wa la quwwata*, the self-subsisting (*al-Qayyum*). How the Quran frames existence before Islamic philosophy formalized the vocabulary.
- [ ] **"The Four Birds and Neoplatonic Emanation: Ibrahim's Question About Death"** (~15min) — Al-Farabi and Plotinus describe existence as emanating from the One. Ibrahim's four birds reassembled from four mountains is the Quran's own treatment of multiplicity resolving back into unity — with the critical difference: the One responds to a request.
- [ ] **"Fitrah: The Primordial Covenant and What It Obligates"** (Izutsu/Baldwin, ~18min, 30:30 + 7:172-173) — The innate religious disposition; the pre-creation testimony; what it means that every soul already said *bala*. Connects to Al-Ghazali's treatment of the heart as a mirror.

### Knowledge and Epistemology
- [ ] **"What the Quran Means by 'Ilm: A Semantic Field Across 750 Occurrences"** (Izutsu, ~20min, long-form) — *'Alima*, *'aql*, *fiqh*, *hikmah*, *'irfan* — the Quran's vocabulary of knowing. Each word inhabits a different epistemological position. How Islamic intellectual traditions grew from these distinctions.
- [ ] **"Tawakkul as a Response to Determinism: Agency in the Prophet Stories"** (Berger/Baldwin, ~18min) — The aqida problem: if Allah decrees all things, what does human effort mean? The prophet stories answer it through action, not argument — each prophet acts fully while trusting completely. Connects to kalam debates on kasb (acquisition of acts).
- [ ] **"Barzakh: The Quran's Three Meanings and What They Share"** (Davenport, ~15min, 23:100, 25:53, 55:20) — Barrier between the living and dead; barrier between two seas; the liminal zone. Ibn Arabi builds an entire ontology on this word. The Quran uses it three times with apparent simplicity. What's actually in the word.

### The Self and Transformation
- [ ] **"Qalb: A Semantic Archaeology of the Quran's Most Transformed Word"** (Izutsu, ~20min, long-form) — *Qalb* means to turn, flip, overturn. The heart is defined by its capacity for reversal. Traces all major Quranic usages: hearts that turn (*taqallab*), hearts turned over (*qallaba*), sealed hearts (*khatama*), hearts that see (*la ta'ma al-absar*). Connects to Al-Ghazali's *Ihya*.
- [ ] **"The Quran's Nafs and the Greek Psyche: A Translation Problem With Theological Stakes"** (Berger, ~15min) — Why translating *nafs* as "soul" imports Greek body/soul dualism the Quran doesn't have. The *nafs* in the Quran is not opposed to the body — it IS the embodied self. Consequences for how we understand death, resurrection, and identity.
